analysis:
- Common strong structure: deterministic Pareto prefilter → compact shortlist (box-volume ± scalarization) → CELF-style lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs → bounded 1-for-1 swap cleanup. Heuristics (1–4,7–10,12–14) follow this and trade HV calls for better selection quality.
- Good cost controls: adaptive shortlist sizes (k‑proportional), selective pairwise HV caching, early pruning by box-volume upper bounds, and explicit HV-call budgets to avoid explosion.
- Weak/expensive patterns: full O(N^2) HV recomputations inside loops, unnecessary global HV recomputes after tiny changes, lack of timestamped lazy updates, and heavy Python-level loops that re-evaluate unchanged marginals.
- Determinism: most good ones use fixed RNG/tie-breakers—important for reproducible search and debugging.
- Missed optimizations across versions: more reuse of computed HV deltas (incremental updates), using tighter inexpensive upper bounds to skip exact HV, and prioritizing candidate testing via approximate HVC (weight-vector approximations) before exact HV.
- Practical tradeoffs: larger shortlists improve solution quality but multiply expensive HV calls; pairwise caching helps only when shortlist is small; swap loops must cap candidate checks per position to keep runtime predictable.
experience: Use an adaptive shortlist (k- and Pareto-aware), compute cheap HVC approximations (many weight vectors) to rank candidates, run CELF with cached singleton HVs + timestamps, prune with tight box-volume/upper bounds, enforce an HV-call budget, and do a capped candidate-limited deterministic swap pass using incremental HV deltas. This yields reproducible, high-quality subsets with predictable runtime.
current_reflection: Use an adaptive shortlist (k- and Pareto-aware), compute cheap HVC approximations (many weight vectors) to rank candidates, run CELF with cached singleton HVs + timestamps, prune with tight box-volume/upper bounds, enforce an HV-call budget, and do a capped candidate-limited deterministic swap pass using incremental HV deltas. This yields reproducible, high-quality subsets with predictable runtime.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prioritize (1) a strong deterministic shortlist: blend normalized rect_vol and scalarized scores, (2) precompute singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with timestamping and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit swaps to top candidates and update sets with O(1) structures, and (5) replace Python O(N^2) dominance checks / hot loops with vectorized or compiled routines and parallel HV evaluations.

Prefer a compact k‑proportional shortlist scored by vectorized box+multiweight scalarization, cache singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with cheap upper‑bound pruning, then do limited deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps. Vectorize nondominated filtering, avoid nested Python loops, only pairwise-cache when shortlist is small, and instrument an HV‑call budget. This yields strong hypervolume with predictable runtime.

Make next‑gen heuristics: (1) rank by cheap proxies to form an adaptive shortlist, (2) cache singleton HVs and use CELF lazy updates, (3) prune with box‑volume/singleton upper bounds and cap HV calls, (4) avoid Python O(N) membership in inner loops (use sets/indices), and (5) only pairwise‑cache when shortlist ≲40. Keep shortlist size adaptive to k and Pareto size.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: adaptive shortlist; Pareto-aware; scalarized HVC approximations (many weights); CELF lazy greedy; cached singleton HVs & timestamps; box-volume upper bounds; HV-call budget; deterministic capped swaps; incremental HV deltas.
- Advice: Form k- and Pareto-sized shortlist via cheap multi-weight HVC proxies, run CELF with cached singleton HVs and timestamps, prune with tight box/singleton upper bounds and an HV-call budget, then do deterministic candidate-capped 1-for-1 swaps using incremental HV deltas; seed RNG and return exactly k.
- Avoid: Nested/HV-heavy Python loops, unbounded HV evaluations, O(N^2) Python dominance checks, large unconditional pairwise caches, and nondeterministic selection.
- Explanation: Minimizes expensive HV calls and uses cheap bounds/caching to produce reproducible, high-quality subsets with predictable runtime.
